title: Beech breadboard with carved feet
subtitle: Flat beech breadboard with chamfered edges and four small carved bun feet
excerpt: A flat beech breadboard with chamfered edges and four small carved bun feet to lift it off the counter. The feet are carved with the sloyd knife from off-cuts of the same beech stock.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management on household bread boards. Cassell's Household Guide on kitchen woodware construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber at stable moisture content. A breadboard in unseasoned wood will warp and crack.
A flat 45-degree bevel along an edge. Chamfered edges on a breadboard reduce chipping and are comfortable to grip.
The direction of the wood fibres. Planing along the grain produces a smooth surface; across the grain produces a rough one.
A knife cut driven away from the body, used to shape the bun feet from small off-cut blocks.

content.text: A breadboard is a flat board used for slicing bread at the table. This version is 35 cm by 25 cm in
seasoned
beech, with
chamfered
edges and four small carved bun feet underneath. The feet are turned-style round pads carved from off-cuts with the sloyd knife using
push cuts
on the round cross-section. Beech is the standard breadboard species: it planes to a very smooth surface along the
grain
, takes a board-butter finish well, and is stable in the warm, slightly damp conditions near a bread bin.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

One board of seasoned beech: 35 cm long, 25 cm wide, 20 mm thick. Four off-cut blocks for the feet: 5 cm by 5 cm by 3 cm each. All pieces should be kiln-dried or thoroughly air-dried beech; beech is the most prone of the common UK hardwoods to movement in damp conditions and must be fully seasoned before use.
Tools
A No. 4 smoothing plane for truing the board face; a block plane for the chamfers; a sloyd knife and leather strop for the feet; a brace and bit for the foot attachment holes; board butter.
Method: the board
Flatten the board face with the No. 4 plane along the grain. Sand through 120, 180, and 240 grit along the grain. The cutting face should be flat and smooth.
Chamfer the top face edges with the block plane:
chamfer
at 45 degrees, 4 mm wide. Work in one direction only on each edge; lifting the plane at the end of each stroke prevents tearout at the corners.
Do not chamfer the underside edges; the square underside edges sit flat when the board is used without feet.
Method: the feet
Shape each foot block to a round cross-section with
push cuts
on the sloyd knife. Target: 35 mm diameter, 20 mm tall. Round the top face to a gentle dome (the bun shape).
Bore a 6 mm hole 10 mm deep in the centre of each foot top face. Bore a matching 6 mm hole 10 mm deep in the board underside at each corner, 4 cm from each edge.
Fit a 6 mm wooden dowel (12 mm long) between each foot and the board: glue the dowel in the foot hole with PVA, let dry, then glue the protruding end into the board hole. Clamp overnight.
Finishing
Apply board butter to all board surfaces: top, underside, edges, and the feet. The full-surface treatment prevents unequal moisture exchange that causes warping.
Care
Rinse under warm water, towel-dry immediately. Never soak or dishwasher. Apply board butter monthly when in daily use. The feet are glued on; protect them from prolonged soaking.
Variations
Larger board for a whole loaf: 45 cm by 30 cm, same construction.
Juice groove: cut a shallow 5 mm groove around the top face perimeter 15 mm from the edge to catch bread crumbs.
No feet version: a flat board without feet is simpler and works equally well on a flat counter.

items.symptom: Board surface is rough in one direction
items.cause: Planing across or against the grain
items.fix: Check the grain direction at the board edge. Plane only in the direction where the plane produces a clean shaving. Across-grain planing requires a cabinet scraper or sanding only.

items.cause: Dowel hole too shallow for the dowel length; or PVA applied when the wood surface was oily
items.fix: Re-glue with fresh PVA after roughing the dowel hole surfaces with coarse sandpaper. Ensure the board surface is clean and dry.

items.symptom: Board warps after a few weeks in the kitchen
items.cause: Top face finished but underside not; unequal moisture exchange
items.fix: Apply board butter to all surfaces equally. Re-flatten the board face if the warp is minor; severe warps need the board to be resawn thinner and re-planed flat.
